station.title: Sudden chest and back pain
station.note: Original practice station aligned to the current AMC format. It is not an AMC examination station or official marking script.

diagnosisTerms: Acute aortic syndrome, particularly aortic dissection

lines.text: You are an emergency medicine registrar working in a metropolitan emergency department.
Mr Julian Merrick, a 59-year-old man, has presented with central chest discomfort and pain between his shoulder blades that began while sitting at a cafe.
Current observations and focused examination findings are available from the examiner if requested.

forbiddenTerms: Acute aortic syndrome, particularly aortic dissection

tasks.text: Explain your most likely diagnosis and important differential diagnoses to the patient.
Address the patient's concerns and explain your management plan.

leadingDiagnosis.text: Acute aortic syndrome, particularly aortic dissection

essentialClues.text: Abrupt severe central chest pain radiating between the shoulder blades and maximal immediately
Inter-arm blood-pressure difference with a weaker left radial pulse
Chronic hypertension; the missed antihypertensive dose is current treatment context and is not asserted as the cause

discriminators.text: Immediate maximum intensity, interscapular radiation and vascular asymmetry favour acute aortic syndrome over ACS, pulmonary embolism and pneumothorax
No current focal neurological deficit or abdominal pain does not remove the high-risk aortic pattern

redFlags.text: Marked inter-arm systolic-pressure and radial-pulse asymmetry

safetyThresholds.text: The high-risk pain pattern plus vascular asymmetry requires an immediate plan for monitored assessment and specialist involvement before confirmation
A patient without circulatory collapse proceeds to urgent CT angiography
The management explanation places heart-rate control before careful blood-pressure reduction while organ perfusion is maintained

criticalActions.text: Explain immediate monitored senior and specialist assessment
Explain strong analgesia and beta-blocker-first anti-impulse treatment under continuous monitoring
Explain urgent CT angiography while the patient is not showing circulatory collapse

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: I would ask the senior emergency doctor to review you now, keep you closely monitored and arrange pain relief while I ask a few essential questions.
criticalActionTimings.rationale: The pulse and blood-pressure asymmetry completes a high-risk acute aortic pattern, so the management explanation cannot be deferred until the end of a prolonged history.

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: With senior supervision and continuous monitoring, we will first use medicine to slow your heart, then lower the blood pressure carefully.
criticalActionTimings.rationale: The assigned management task requires a safe explanation of analgesia and beta-blocker-first anti-impulse treatment under monitored senior care.

criticalActionTimings.positiveActionPhrase: As you are not showing signs of circulatory collapse, the main test is an urgent CT angiogram.
criticalActionTimings.rationale: A high-risk patient without circulatory collapse requires urgent definitive aortic imaging without delay from lower-yield testing.

unsafeActions.text: Do not give routine aspirin or fibrinolysis while aortic dissection is strongly suspected
Do not lower blood pressure before heart-rate control in the explained anti-impulse sequence

scopeBoundary.text: The candidate explains the immediate monitored plan, analgesia, anti-impulse treatment, imaging and specialist disposition without enacting unrequested emergency-department procedures

identityResponse.text: My name is Julian Merrick, and my date of birth is 7 September 1966.

initialVoluntary.text: Half an hour ago, while I was sitting at a cafe, severe pain suddenly struck the centre of my chest and shot between my shoulder blades. It was at its worst immediately. I became sweaty, felt sick and nearly fainted. The pain has not eased.

concerns.text: Whether the pain is indigestion after spicy food
concerns.responseText: I had a spicy lunch. Could this just be indigestion?
Are you saying the artery has torn?

concerns.text: How serious the possible diagnosis is
Whether aspirin should be given because his father died from a heart attack
concerns.responseText: My father died from a heart attack. Should I have aspirin?

concerns.text: Whether medicine will fix the problem
concerns.responseText: Will those medicines fix the problem?

concerns.text: Whether an operation will be needed
Whether his partner can accompany him
concerns.responseText: My partner is in the waiting room. Can they come with me?

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about current pain location.
disclosures.responseText: It is still in the middle of my chest and between my shoulder blades.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about pain character.
disclosures.responseText: It feels deep and tearing. I have never felt anything like it.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about current pain severity.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about loss of consciousness.
disclosures.responseText: I nearly fainted but stayed conscious.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about effect of deep breathing.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about breathlessness.
disclosures.responseText: Only a little because the pain frightens me.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about focal weakness.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about focal numbness.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about speech disturbance.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about abdominal pain.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about known aortic enlargement.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about medical history.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about regular medicines.
disclosures.responseText: Irbesartan, but I missed it today.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about current blood-thinning medicine.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about medicine allergies.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

disclosures.triggerDescription: The Doctor makes one focused request about understanding of the plan.
disclosures.responseText: You are worried there may be a problem in my main artery, but it is not confirmed. I need close monitoring, pain and blood-pressure treatment, an urgent scan and review by the surgical team.
disclosures.responseUse: The next Doctor turn incorporates the answer into the focused assessment or management plan.

releases.responseText: Pulse 108 regular, respiratory rate 22, oxygen saturation 97 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees Celsius. Blood pressure is 198 over 110 in the right arm and 166 over 96 in the left.
releases.findingSource: Current emergency-department observations available from the Examiner as stated in the Stem.
releases.responseUse: The Doctor obtains consent for focused examination after recognising tachycardia, severe hypertension and inter-arm pressure asymmetry.

releases.requestDescription: After obtaining consent, the Doctor requests the focused examination findings available from the Examiner.
releases.requestExactText: Please provide the focused cardiovascular, respiratory, neurological and peripheral vascular examination findings.
releases.responseText: The left radial pulse is weaker. Heart sounds are dual without a murmur. Air entry is equal and the lungs are clear. He is alert, with normal speech, symmetrical facial movement, equal limb power and no sensory loss. Peripheries are warm; capillary refill is two seconds.
releases.findingSource: Focused cardiovascular, respiratory, neurological and peripheral-vascular findings available from the Examiner as stated in the Stem.
releases.responseUse: The Doctor recognises vascular asymmetry and immediately explains the need for monitored senior review before completing essential questions.

scheduledPrompts.speaker: Examiner
scheduledPrompts.responseText: Three minutes have passed. Please proceed to the remaining tasks.
scheduledPrompts.markingConsequence: The Doctor proceeds to the remaining tasks; the prompt itself carries no performance inference.

closing.text: Thank you. The station is complete.

keySteps.description: Elicits abrupt maximum-at-onset chest-to-back pain and identifies vascular asymmetry
Recognises the high-risk acute aortic pattern and explains the need for monitored senior review before diagnostic confirmation
Explains the leading acute aortic diagnosis, uncertainty and important dangerous alternatives
Explains safe analgesia, heart-rate-first anti-impulse treatment, definitive imaging and specialist disposition

domains.name: Choice and technique of examination, organisation and sequence

projectSafetyErrors.description: Completes a prolonged history before explaining monitored senior review despite the high-risk aortic pattern
Recommends routine aspirin or fibrinolytic treatment before dissection is excluded
Explains blood-pressure reduction before heart-rate control
Allows lower-yield tests to delay definitive aortic imaging

globalRating.decisionRule: The global rating alone determines the station result: ratings 1 to 3 fail and ratings 4 to 7 pass.
globalRating.passStandard: Completes the assigned assessment and explanations; recognises the high-risk acute aortic pattern; and clearly explains monitored review, imaging, heart-rate-first treatment and specialist involvement without enacting unrequested procedures or recommending unsafe empirical aspirin or fibrinolysis.
globalRating.failStandard: Misses the acute aortic pattern, recommends unsafe aspirin or fibrinolysis, reverses the heart-rate-first sequence, enacts unrequested procedures or substantially fails the assigned tasks.

identity.verificationText: Hello, I am Ushana, one of the doctors. Could you confirm your full name and date of birth?
identity.rationale: The patient directly confirms full name and date of birth before consultation consent and clinical questioning.

consents.requestText: Julian, is it okay if I ask you some questions about the pain?

variation.distinctJob: Recognise acute aortic syndrome from abrupt maximum-at-onset chest-to-back pain plus vascular asymmetry, then explain the immediate safe plan and why empirical aspirin or fibrinolysis is withheld.
variation.newSurprise: The pain began while quietly sitting at a cafe rather than during exertion, and the patient's spicy-meal explanation competes with the vascular pattern.
variation.plannedTrap: Anchoring on indigestion or routine ACS treatment can delay definitive aortic imaging or expose the patient to harmful aspirin or fibrinolysis.
antiFailureFeatures: The Doctor explains monitored senior review once abrupt maximum-intensity pain and vascular asymmetry establish a high-risk aortic pattern.
The Doctor explains why aspirin is withheld while retaining acute coronary syndrome as an important alternative.
The Doctor explains heart-rate control before careful blood-pressure reduction without enacting treatment.

duplicateCheck.conclusion: This case tests acute aortic syndrome with vascular asymmetry and aspirin or fibrinolysis hazard. Current Case 1 tests non-classic acute coronary syndrome, while the Emergency Explore comparisons use different presentations and performance jobs.

requiredEvidence.requirement: A focused assessment is visibly conducted
The focused assessment integrates the dangerous aortic pattern
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: Your pain began suddenly at maximum severity, spread from your chest to your back and nearly caused fainting.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: Your pain began suddenly at maximum severity, spread from your chest to your back and nearly caused fainting.

requiredEvidence.requirement: The most likely diagnosis is explicitly explained
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: my main concern is an aortic dissection.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: my main concern is an aortic dissection.

requiredEvidence.requirement: Important differential diagnoses are explicitly explained
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: Other important diagnoses are a heart attack, a lung clot and a collapsed lung.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: Other important diagnoses are a heart attack, a lung clot and a collapsed lung.

requiredEvidence.requirement: The patient's concerns are addressed within the immediate management plan
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: A staff member can update your partner
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: A staff member can update your partner

requiredEvidence.requirement: The management plan explains heart-rate-first anti-impulse treatment
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: With senior supervision and continuous monitoring, we will first use medicine to slow your heart, then lower the blood pressure carefully.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: With senior supervision and continuous monitoring, we will first use medicine to slow your heart, then lower the blood pressure carefully.

requiredEvidence.requirement: The management plan includes definitive aortic imaging
requiredEvidence.exactPhrase: As you are not showing signs of circulatory collapse, the main test is an urgent CT angiogram.
requiredEvidence.positivePerformancePhrase: As you are not showing signs of circulatory collapse, the main test is an urgent CT angiogram.

listenTest.reviewer: FFmpeg libflite synthetic speech timing rehearsal
listenTest.notes: A reproducible line-by-line synthetic spoken rehearsal rendered 417.44 seconds of all visible non-Action lines. Reviewed Action intervals add 8 seconds; the raw total is 425.44 seconds and the recorded whole-second total is 425 seconds. The three-minute prompt starts at 182.73 seconds. This verifies elapsed-time feasibility only, not dialogue naturalness. The legacy spoken_readthrough method identifier is retained for Engine 1.1 schema compatibility; no human listening event is claimed. Evidence: audits/CASE_002_TIMING_EVIDENCE.json sha256:b4313443e43f033e0349f298018890199cb0744677548e385ea3dd7d4113066c.

claimMap.claim: Abrupt severe chest or back pain that is maximal at onset, syncope or presyncope, pulse deficit and a marked inter-arm blood-pressure difference are high-risk features of acute aortic syndrome.

sourceLocators.locator: Clinical assessment: pain features, pulse deficit, blood-pressure differential, neurological deficit and syncope.

claimMap.claim: Acute coronary syndrome, pulmonary embolism and pneumothorax remain important dangerous chest-pain alternatives, but immediate maximum intensity, interscapular radiation and vascular asymmetry favour acute aortic syndrome.

sourceLocators.locator: Comprehensive guideline, history and assessment of presenting complaint: targeted time-critical chest-pain differentials.
Clinical assessment: abrupt pain, pulse deficit, blood-pressure differential and syncope as high-risk aortic features.

claimMap.claim: A high-risk suspected acute aortic dissection requires immediate monitored emergency care with early emergency-senior, intensive-care, radiology and cardiothoracic or vascular specialist involvement.

sourceLocators.locator: Management of suspected aortic dissection: resuscitation, monitoring and urgent specialist consultation.

claimMap.claim: CT angiography is the preferred definitive test for a haemodynamically stable patient with suspected acute aortic dissection and should not be delayed by lower-yield testing.

sourceLocators.locator: Investigations: CT angiography as preferred imaging in the haemodynamically stable patient.

claimMap.claim: Use CT angiography when the patient is stable; if too unstable for CT, urgent bedside echocardiography can identify complications and guide the team, but a negative bedside study does not reliably exclude dissection.

sourceLocators.locator: Investigations: CT angiography for stable patients and bedside TTE or TOE pathway when unstable.
Full guideline linked from the page, diagnostic imaging recommendations for suspected acute aortic syndrome.

claimMap.claim: Treat pain and reduce heart rate and blood pressure under continuous monitoring; the joint ACC/AHA guideline recommends intravenous beta-blocker-first therapy and adding a vasodilator only after heart-rate control if pressure remains above target.

sourceLocators.locator: Management: general heart-rate and blood-pressure reduction targets for suspected aortic dissection.
Full guideline linked from the page, acute medical management recommendations for acute aortic syndromes.

claimMap.claim: For acute aortic syndrome, target a heart rate of 60 to 80 beats per minute and systolic pressure below 120 millimetres of mercury or the lowest pressure that maintains organ perfusion.

sourceLocators.locator: Management targets: heart rate 60 to 80 and systolic pressure 100 to 120 using the higher-arm reading initially.
Full guideline linked from the page, acute aortic syndrome blood-pressure and heart-rate targets.

claimMap.claim: In this high-risk presentation, the ACI warning to consider dissection before antiplatelet or fibrinolytic treatment supports withholding empirical aspirin and fibrinolysis until dissection is excluded or the specialist team advises otherwise.

sourceLocators.locator: Safety warning to consider aortic dissection before antiplatelet or fibrinolytic treatment.

claimMap.claim: Acute ascending aortic dissection requires emergency surgical evaluation, while uncomplicated descending dissection is initially managed medically with intervention for rupture, malperfusion, extension, enlargement, intractable pain or uncontrolled hypertension.

sourceLocators.locator: Definitive management of Stanford Type A and Type B dissection.
Full guideline linked from the page, recommendations for acute Type A and acute Type B dissection.

claimMap.claim: The station uses the current AMC two-minute reading and eight-minute assessment format, predominant assessment area, observable tasks, domains and seven-point global rating threshold.

sourceLocators.locator: Clinical Examination Specifications V8.3, station structure, timing and task sections.
Assessment Domains V1.1, domain definitions and performance expectations.
